passage: An education nonprofit analyzed standardized test scores across school districts grouped by per-pupil spending levels. The organization reported that districts with higher per-pupil spending tended to have higher average test scores.
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the figure to support the organization's report?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Districts spending over $15,000 per pupil averaged a score of 82, compared to 71 for districts spending $10,000-$15,000 and 58 for those spending under $10,000.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Districts spending under $10,000 per pupil had an average score of 58, which was the lowest among all spending categories.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Districts spending $10,000-$15,000 per pupil averaged a score of 71, which was 13 points higher than the lowest-spending category.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The highest-spending districts averaged a score of 82, while the middle-spending districts averaged 71, a difference of 11 points.

explanation: The claim is that higher spending is associated with higher scores. Choice A supports this by showing the full progression: under $10K = 58, $10K-$15K = 71, over $15K = 82 -- a clear positive association across all three levels. Choice B only mentions the lowest group. Choice C compares only two of three categories and focuses on a point difference rather than the overall trend. Choice D omits the lowest group, failing to show the full correlation.

distractorRationale.A: Correct: cites all three spending levels and their corresponding scores, demonstrating a consistent positive association.
distractorRationale.B: Wrong (incomplete evidence): only cites the lowest spending group's score without showing how it compares across the full range of spending levels.
distractorRationale.C: Wrong (misread value): compares only two groups and focuses on a point difference rather than the overall trend across all categories.
distractorRationale.D: Wrong (incomplete evidence): omits the lowest spending group, so the reader cannot see whether the positive trend holds across all levels.

imageDescription: Bar chart titled 'Average Test Scores by Per-Pupil Spending Level.' X-axis: Spending Category (Under $10,000; $10,000-$15,000; Over $15,000). Y-axis: Average Test Score (0-100). Bars: Under $10,000 = 58, $10,000-$15,000 = 71, Over $15,000 = 82. Source: National Education Research Nonprofit, 2024.

chartData.title: Average Test Scores by Per-Pupil Spending Level

passageMetadata.source: Adapted from National Education Research Nonprofit annual findings
